title: Lesson 369
transcript: There's two additional types of testing that we need to talk about here, decision table analysis and state-based analysis. These are both fairly specialized types of testing that you'll use in certain scenarios. Let's start with decision table analysis. The main reason that you would use this type of testing is if you have an application that has quite a few different inputs and therefore quite a few different outputs, And what you're testing for here is that it can quickly become very difficult to test an application and make sure you've tested all the possible combinations. So let me give you a really simple example, our little flashcard app. It's an incredibly simple app. but it still has a few options. You can go into the menu and you can decide, do you want to see terms first or definitions first when you go to the next card? So that's one thing you'd have to test. The next thing is you have kind of three options. Do you want to see cards that you haven't seen yet? Do you want to see cards that you've marked as don't know? Do you want to see cards that you marked as don't know? Even with those just few options, right? Do you want term first or definition first? Do you want to see only cards that you don't know or only cards that you do know or cards that you do know and the ones that you haven't seen yet? Even with just these few options, you can see there's suddenly all these different combinations that we have to test. All right, we have to test term first, only cards we haven't seen. Term first, only cards we've marked as we know. Definition first, you know. You can see how this suddenly gets very complicated. And that's the whole point of decision table analysis. You can build a giant table that shows all these various input combinations and you can make sure that you've got really good coverage with your testing. You've covered all the possible combinations. So that's what decision table analysis is for. State-based analysis is particularly useful when you want to test an application or a protocol or a system that can exist in different states. So let me give you a great example. The TCPIP protocol. This is a protocol John's going to talk about in great detail in Domain 4. This is what's referred to as a connection-oriented protocol. And there's kind of like three major states that a TCP IP connection can exist in. The first state is when you're establishing the connection. And as John will talk about, when you want to establish a TCP IP connection, you have to go through what's called the three-way handshake. The first system has to send a synchronized packet. The second system then sends back a SYN ACK, like I acknowledge, I want to synchronize as well. And then the first system replies with an ACK. This is called the three-way handshake. And so if you're testing TCPIP, the first state that you would test it in is in this handshake and this establishment of the protocol phase. Once the connection has been established, you now need to do different testing because now you're in the phase where data can be sent back and forth. And then there's even a third phase where you're tearing down the connection. And so the idea here is that lots of systems, lots of different protocols will exist in different states and they act differently in different states. So you obviously need to modify your testing based on the state that the system is in. And that's the whole idea behind state-based analysis.
